coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 in aqueous phase using amino-functionalized MOFs loaded with hydroxy-functionalized graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Hydroxy-functionalized graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs) were loaded onto NH2-MIL-101(Fe) to prepare a novel binary OH-GQDs/NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (OGNM) composite with a simple solvothermal method. The obtained catalysts were characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, FI-IR, XPS and BET to analyze the morphology, elemental composition, and specific surface area of the composite material. Notably, the morphology and micro-structures of OGMM-3 exhibited that OH-GQDs evenly dispersed on the surface of hexagonal micropillar NH2-MIL-101(Fe). The nanocomposite catalysts were tested in a photocatalytic CO2 reduction system using water and acetonitrile as solvents, without additional sacrificial agents. OGNM-3 composite possessed the optimum photoreduction CO2 activity, with a CO yield of 128.68 μmol·g−1 within 10 h, which was 5.3 times higher than that of pure NH2-MIL-101(Fe). After three cycles of reuse, the catalyst still maintained catalytic activity without any significant changes in its crystal structure, morphology or functional groups. In summary, this study proposed a design for synthesizing binary OGNM photocatalysts, offering useful insights for cost-effectively address CO2 emissions in greenhouse gas emissions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydroxy-functionalized graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs) were loaded onto NH2-MIL-101(Fe) to prepare a novel binary OH-GQDs/NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (OGNM) composite with a simple solvothermal method. The obtained catalysts were characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, FI-IR, XPS and BET to analyze the morphology, elemental composition, and specific surface area of the composite material. Notably, the morphology and micro-structures of OGMM-3 exhibited that OH-GQDs evenly dispersed on the surface of hexagonal micropillar NH2-MIL-101(Fe). The nanocomposite catalysts were tested in a photocatalytic CO2 reduction system using water and acetonitrile as solvents, without additional sacrificial agents. OGNM-3 composite possessed the optimum photoreduction CO2 activity, with a CO yield of 128.68 μmol·g−1 within 10 h, which was 5.3 times higher than that of pure NH2-MIL-101(Fe). After three cycles of reuse, the catalyst still maintained catalytic activity without any significant changes in its crystal structure, morphology or functional groups. In summary, this study proposed a design for synthesizing binary OGNM photocatalysts, offering useful insights for cost-effectively address CO2 emissions in greenhouse gas emissions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Climate change and excessive carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions have significant concerns stems from their detrimental impact on ecosystems [1].Various countries and international organizations actively work towards implementing initiatives and policies to combat reduce CO2 emissions and energy scarcity [2], and a collaborative effort among many researchers is also focused on tackling these urgent environmental challenges, aiming for a global shift towards a sustainable future [3]. In recent years, researchers have discovered an intriguing technique known as photocatalytic reduction of CO2, which effectively converts CO2 into valuable carbon contained chemicals and fuels [4,5].

In 1979, INOUE et al initially reported a photosensitive semiconductor as a photocatalyst to reduce CO2 under light irradiation [6]. Since then, various photocatalytic hybridized composites such as semiconductor photocatalysts [7], metal nanoparticles [8], metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have been developed and utilized for CO2 photoreduction [9,10]. It is noteworthy that MOFs are a fascinating class of materials, with highly porous crystalline structures, result from connecting metal ions/clusters through organic ligands [11,12]. Moreover, MOFs can be tailored by changing metal ions, organic ligands, and combined with other nanomaterials to suit the needs in many fields [13–15]. For instance, well-known iron-based MOFs have garnered extensive research interest due to their abundance of iron oxide clusters, such as MIL-53(Fe), MIL-101(Fe) and MIL-88(Fe) [13,16,17], and the introduction of amino groups onto the organic linker could enhance the photostability [18,19]. Numerous MOFs-based composites have demonstrated the stable and promising application potential in degradation [20,21], adsorption [22,23] and photocatalysis [24,25].

Returning to photocatalytic CO2 reduction, based on our previous research, it is necessary to add additional sacrificial triethanolamine as an electron donor in the photocatalytic reaction system [16]. To the best of our knowledge, there are limited studies reporting on MOFs utilized in a water/acetonitrile system (water acts as electron donor instead of sacrificial agent) for CO2 photoreduction, which is a motivation for us in pursuing research on the development of photocatalysts and catalytic system. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a highly intriguing material, boasting numerous novel chemical and physical properties, such as high charge carrier mobility, better surface grafting and excellent solubility, making them as ideal candidates for incorporation into various composite materials [26,27]. Therefore, GQDs decorated MOFs offers a promising strategy for developing advanced functional materials.

In this study, hydroxyl-functionalized GQDs (OH-GQDs) was introduced on the surface of amino-functionalized MIL-101(Fe) (NH2-MIL-101(Fe)) to synthesize a novel Z-type heterojunction photocatalyst OH-GQDs/NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (OGNM) by a facile solvothermal synthesis method, and the related characterization analysis was carried out to investigate the microcrystalline structure, chemical composition, surface morphology of the material. The incorporation of OH-GQDs onto MOFs could therefore synergistically exploit the unique properties of both materials, leading to enhanced performance in various applications. The photocatalytic reduction of CO2 under the visible light at normal temperature and pressure was explored, which made it more likely to be applied in practice.

All chemicals and solvents used in this study were of commercially available analytical grade and obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., (China), without the need for any additional treatment.

The preparation method of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was obtained by further optimizing the related synthesis methods [28,29]. Specific steps were as follows: Firstly, dissolved ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-H2BDC) into 10 mL N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) solution with a molar ratio of 2:1. Then, put the mixed solution into an autoclave and crystallized at 120 °C for 24 h. After solvothermal treatment, the brown powder was collected, washed repeatedly with DMF and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain crystalline NH2-MIL-101(Fe).

In this study, OH-GQDs with modifications were prepared using a water-phase molecular fusion method [30]. C16H10 (1 g) was nitrationized in nitric acid (80 mL, 80 °C) for 12 h. After diluted with deionized water (1 L), the solution was filtered to remove impurities. Then, the filtered solution was dispersed in 0.2 M NaOH sonicated for 2 h. Subsequently, transferred the reactants into a closed polytetrafluoroethylene high-pressure reactor and heated at 200 °C for 10 h and then filtered again. The filtrate was dialyzed for 2 days and dried at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain OH-GQDs for subsequent experiments.

The OGNM composites were prepared by a solvothermal synthesis method similar to 2.1. Weighed a certain mass of FeCl3·6H2O (2.48 mmol) and NH2-H2BDC (1.24 mmol) into 10 mL of DMF solution, and continued to stir the mixed solution at room temperature for 15 min to form a homogeneous mixture. Then, a given mass of the OH-GQDs solution was slowly added dropwise with stirring and sonicating for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene high-pressure reactor and crystallized at 120 °C for 20 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was washed with DMF and ethanol for several times. The dark brown product obtained through centrifugation was then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The final products were marked as OGNM-1, OGNM-2, OGNM-3 and OGNM-4 due to the mass content of OH-GQDs in the composite material was1 wt%, 4 wt%, 7 wt% and 10 wt%, respectively.
Details of characterization instruments were shown in the Text S1.

A 150 mL of stirred polytetrafluoroethylene reactor sealed with a steel flanges was employed to test the photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of the prepared samples under simulated sunlight. First, 60 mg of the powder photocatalyst was put into water/acetonitrile and sealed into reactor. Then, the system was filled with high-purity CO2 gas for 30 min to exclude air. The solar irradiation was simulated by 300 W Xe lamp (420 < λ < 800 nm). The system was carried out in normal pressure and ambient temperature. Finally, after being irradiated, gas chromatograph (GC 2060, with methanizer) equipped with FID detector and column (C18) was used to detect the photoreduction gas products.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images was used to investigate the morphology and micro-structures of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) before and after loading OH-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, NH2-MIL-101(Fe) exhibits a regular and well-defined hexagonal micropillar crystals structure, which is similar to the morphology reported in the literature [31]. The morphology of OGNM-3 composite can be seen from Fig. 1b that when the OH-GQDs doping with NH2-MIL-101(Fe), the MOFs as a base still maintains the hexagonal micropillar crystals morphology, and the OH-GQDs were closely loaded on its surface without agglomeration. In Fig. 1c and d, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping images show a percentage composition and distribution of the main elements presented throughout the composite (including C, O, Fe and N). It is obvious that OH-GQDs are uniformly dispersed on the surface of NH2-MIL-101(Fe), indicating the successful synthesis of OH-GQDs and NH2-MIL-101 (Fe) composite. To further confirm the internal structure of the dense interface formed between OH-GQDs and NH2-MIL-101, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were conducted. Fig. 1e depicts the TEM pattern of OH-GQDs, exhibiting a uniform dispersion of OH-GQDs. The TEM pattern of OGNM-3 in Fig. 1f shows the composite still maintains the hexagonal micropillar crystal structure of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OH-GQDs uniformly distribute on the surface of NH2-MIL-101(Fe), which is consistent with the SEM image. It is noteworthy to mention that OH-GQDs were successfully loading on the surface of NH2-MIL-101 with an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1g) [32]. These findings suggest that the OGNM-3 composite is successfully synthesized by OH-GQDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe) using one-step solvothermal method, and the clear interface between the two materials is conducive to providing a path for the conduction of electron transfer.

The crystallographic structure of pure NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and a series of OGNM nanocomposites were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 2 a). The XRD pattern of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) displays prominent diffraction peaks within the 2θ range of 5-25°, which are similar to the typical main diffraction peaks of MIL-101(Fe) structures [33,34]. Furthermore, by examining the XRD patterns of the OGNM composites, it is apparent that the main characteristic peaks of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) in the composites are retained, signifying that the introduction of OH-GQDs maintained the crystal structure of the original NH2-MIL-101(Fe). However, it is observed that the characteristic peaks of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) weakened with the increase in doping amount of OH-GQDs in OGNM composites. These results can be attributed to the fact that the high loading content of OH-GQDs in the composite affects the crystallization of MOFs [31].

Fig. 2b illustrates the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OGNM composites to study their molecular structure and functional groups. The peak observed at 1251 cm−1 in NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OGNM composites could be assigned to the characteristic C-N stretch of aromatic amines [34]. The asymmetric and symmetrical stretching vibrational infrared peaks of the O-C-O framework are strong bands existed in the 1400–1600 cm−1 region, which are characteristic peaks of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) [35]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1578 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 can be attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric infrared vibration peaks of the carboxyl group [36]. The absorption peak at approximately 768 cm−1 assigns to Fe-O groups [37]. Furthermore, the two bands at 3325 cm−1 and 3466 cm−1 are attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrational infrared peaks of the N–H group, it can be clearly seen that there are amino groups in the OGNM catalysts [33,38]. In addition, a broad infrared absorption peak gradually appeared at around 3400 cm−1 in the composite, which corresponds to the infrared vibration peak of O–H [30], and with the increasing amount of OH-GQDs, the O–H signal is gradually enhanced. This suggested that both OH-GQDs and NH2-MIL-101(Fe) exist in the OGNM composites.

Based on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, the surface chemical composition and elemental states of the prepared nanocomposites were further investigated. The XPS spectra measurements indicate the presence of Fe, C, N, and O elements in both pure NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (as shown in Fig. S1) and OGNM-3 composite material (as shown in Fig. 2c-f). The C 1 s XPS spectrum of OGNM-3 exhibits binding energies at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.6 eV, consistent with those of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) materials, attributed to the benzene ring of the MOFs organic linker, C-O/C-N species, and carboxylate groups, respectively [39,40]. For OGNM-3, it is worth to note that a peak at 288.2 eV represents the C-OH bonds of OH-GQDs [41]. The O 1 s XPS spectra of OGNM-3 and pure NH2-MIL-101(Fe) exhibit the peaks at 530.4 eV and 531.6 eV, corresponding to Fe-O in the MOF and C = O, respectively [42]. Furthermore, a peak at 531.4 eV is observed in the OGNM-3 catalyst, confirming the presence of carboxylate groups from OH-GQD loaded onto the MOFs material [43,44]. Additionally, 533.0 eV represents adsorbed oxygen on the surface [45]. Fig. 2f shows the high resolution spectrum of Fe 2p of OGNM-3 nanocomposites, the peaks at binding energy of 712.5 and 725.6 eV mainly correspond to Fe(III) at Fe 2p 3/2 and Fe 2p ½ [46], respectively. Compared with NH2-MIL-101(Fe), the characteristic peaks of Fe 2p was observed to shift slightly toward the lower binding energy, indicating the presence of an electron-inductive effect between OH-GQDs and Fe species [45,47]. XPS results suggest that photocatalytic composite interaction between the OH-GQDs and NH2-MIL-101 were uniformly bonded together.

The N2 absorption–desorption isotherms were conducted to determine the pore structures and specific surface areas of the as-prepared catalysts. As shown in Fig. 3 a, OGNM-4 presents a type II isotherm [48], suggesting the existence of micropores within the composites. Whereas the isotherms of pristine NH2-MIL-101(Fe), OGNM-1, OGNM-2 and OGNM-3 are between type I and type IV according to the IUPAC classifications [49], indicating the presence of both micropores and mesopores in composites [50]. The results are consistent with the pore size distributions of the prepared materials in Fig. 3b. The decrease of specific surface area for OGNM composite materials is mainly ascribed to the loading of OH-GQDs (Table S1), which occupy the partial surface of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and thus form heterojunctions between NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OH-GQDs. Moreover, the appreciable capability of CO2 adsorption of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OGNM-3 at 273 K was also investigated in Fig. S2. The OGNM-3 exhibits adsorption behavior quite similar to NH2-MIL-101(Fe), with capacities of 0.81 mmol·g−1 and 0.89 mmol·g−1 at 273 K and 1 bar, respectively. The NH2-MOF-101 loaded with OH-GQDs displays a steeper adsorption isotherm in the low pressure region (0 to 0.15 atm) for CO2 uptake compared to the bare NH2-MOF-101(Fe), and exhibits the higher CO2 adsorption capacity of 0.43 mmol·g−1 at 0.15 bar (Fig. 3c). It could be noticed that the introduction of OH-GQD improves CO2 affinity in the low-pressure region.

The pure MOFs and binary composites were tested for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 using water/acetonitrile mixed solution (10 mL) as reaction medium, without any sacrificial agent, in a photocatalytic reactor. Firstly, a control experiment was conducted to directly measure the carbon-containing gas products generated from photocatalytic CO2 reduction. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, in the absence of light or photocatalysts, no reduction products is detected in the photocatalytic reduction reaction, indicating that visible light irradiation and photocatalysts are essential for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2. Additionally, it is found that when nitrogen atmosphere replaced CO2, no carbonaceous gas is detected, suggesting that CO2 is the sole source of carbonaceous products in the photocatalytic reduction system. Notably, employing MIL-101(Fe) as the photocatalyst, with water/acetonitrile as solvents, successfully achieved CO2 photocatalytic reduction to CO when triethanolamine was added as a sacrificial agent [16]. However, in this case, MIL-101(Fe) did not undergo photocatalytic reactions without the addition of sacrificial agents. Conversely, NH2-MIL-101(Fe) showed the detection of CO as a product during photocatalytic CO2 reduction under the same conditions, indicating the significant role of amino functional groups in enhancing catalytic activity.

Fig. 4b and c demonstrates the CO2 conversion activities over different as-obtained photocatalysts. The conversion of CO2 to CO increased almost linearly with the irradiation time as shown in Fig. 4b. Among all the photocatalysts, OGNM-3 exhibits the best activity of CO2 conversion to CO, and the yield of CO reaches 128.7 µmol·g−1 with the irradiation time of 10 h. Meanwhile, the pristine NH2-MIL-101(Fe) displays the negligible CO evolution compared with OGNM-3, which might be assigned to the rapid combination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, the CO production is 12.8 µmol·g−1·h−1 for OGNM-3, which is approximately 5.3 times higher than the pure NH2-MOFs (2.4 μmol·g−1·h−1). It can be seen that as the addition amount of OH-GQDs increases, the photocatalytic reduction activity shows an initial increasing trend and then decreases. When an excess quantity of OH-GQDs is added, it tends to aggregate on the surface of MOFs substrate, making it challenging to achieve uniform dispersion. These results in a reduction of the space available for capturing photogenerated electrons, ultimately leading to a decrease in photocatalytic activity [51]. Overall, the results show that the introduction of OH-GQDs enhances the photoreduction performance of NH2-MIL-101(Fe), and adding an appropriate amount of OH-GQDs is more effective.

The recycling and stability of photocatalytic materials are indispensable part during practical application, thus the recycling experiments of OGNM-3 was conducted and recorded in Fig. 4d. Three recycling runs were carried out over OGNM-3 nanocomposite under the same experimental conditions, and the samples were not subjected to additional cleaning procedures, which were directly dried and reused. The results show that there is no significant reduction of the yield of CO after three runs. Moreover, the XRD (Fig. 4e) and FI-IR (Fig. S3e) patterns of OGNM-3 after three runs are the same as the fresh one, confirming the high photostability of the OGNM composite under visible light. For the XPS analysis of reused OGNM-3(Fig. S3a-d), a notable change is observed in the O 1 s spectrum, where the content of adsorbed oxygen on the surface increases from 12.8 % to 18.9 % (as indicated by the peak at 533.0 eV) [45,52]. Coupled with analysis of the Fe 2p, electron donors from O2– contributes to the accelerated Fe2+/Fe3+ cycling [53]. The peaks at 711.2 and 724.3 eV are attributed to Fe(II), whereas the peaks at 712.6 and 725.6 eV correspond to Fe(III), respectively [42,47], indicating an increase in the Fe3+ content from 58 % to 67 %. The enhancement of oxygen concentration in the reaction typically supplements additional oxidants, thereby facilitating the conversion of Fe2+to Fe3+ [42]. SEM image for OGNM-3 after reactions is shown in Fig. 4f. It can be observed that there is almost no change in the morphology of the OGNM-3 sample before (Fig. 1b) and after the catalyst reaction, further indicating the stability of the catalyst under the reaction conditions.

The optical properties of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OGNM nanomaterials were evaluated by UV–vis-DRS (Fig. 5 a). All samples display a strong light absorption edge in the visible region. On the one hand, NH2-MIL-101(Fe) own light absorption with a band at 300–500 nm due to its amino ligands and Fe3+-O clusters [54]. The introduction of OH-GQDs on MOF samples result in a slight red-shift in the light absorption edges and a significant increase in the light absorption intensity within 500–800 nm, indicating that the doping of OH-GQDs further enhances the light response. The bandgap (Eg) was calculated from the tangent of the (αhν)2 versus light energy (hν) Tauc plots, as shown in Fig. 5b. The Eg values of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OGNM-3 are determined to be 2.16 eV and 1.80 eV, respectively. It shows that the introduction of OH-GQDs narrows the bandgap of NH2-MOFs, which facilitates the transition of electrons from the valence band to get enhancement photogenerated carrier transfer activity. As anticipated, the photoreduction activity is consistent with the UV–vis DRS and bandgap plots analyzed previously, the combination between the NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OH-GQDs made the photogenerated carriers of the material more prone to transition.

To further investigate the process of photogenerated electron-hole pair separation and interfacial transfer during the photocatalytic reaction, photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) and electrochemical characterization were performed. The PL spectra of the NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and OGNM-3 samples were shown in Fig. 5c. NH2-MOFs exhibits a higher PL signals compared to the binary composite material OGNM-3, indicating that loading of OH-GQDs on NH2-MIL-101(Fe) could effectively increase the charge separation efficiency and elevate the photoreduction performance. Moreover, the transient photocurrent density of the binary composite OGNM-3 is significantly higher than that of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (Fig. 5d), which indicates OGNM-3 possesses higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes [55]. The arc radius of OGNM-3 is smaller than that of pure NH2-MIL-101(Fe) (Fig. 5e), indicating lower interfacial resistance for OGNM-3, which is in line with the PL analysis and the transient photocurrent density results. The enhanced photogenerated charge separation activity of OGNM-3 could be attributed to the formation of heterojunction between OH-GQDs and NH2-MOFs, and thus improves its CO2 photocatalytic reduction activity.

Fig. 5f shows the Mott-Schottky (MS) curve of OGNM-3 and NH2-MIL-101(Fe), the slope of the fitted straight line of the MS plot is positive, the intersection of the x-axis represents the flat band position. The conductive band (CB) of OGNM-3 is located at -0.96 eV vs the normalized hydrogen electrode (NHE). On the basis of Tauc plot, the bandgap of OGNM-3 is 1.80 eV, and thus the valence band (VB) can be estimated as 0.84 eV vs NHE. Obviously, the CB of OGNM-3 is more negative than the CO2/CO reduction potential (CO2 + 2H++2Ecb- → CO + H2O, E0 = -0.53 V), and the VB of the binary OGNM-3 is more positive than the oxidation site of O2/H2O (H2O → 1/2O2 + 2H++2e-, E0 = +0.82 V vs. NHE) [56]. It can be clearly found that with the loading of OH-GQDs on NH2-MIL-101(Fe), the CB and VB potentials become more negative and more positive, respectively, which means that the composite OGNM-3 composite have potential for stronger driving force CO2 reduction [57,58]. Moreover, the bandgap of the OGNM-3 composite material became narrower, providing excellent light-harvesting ability [35]. The band structure of all synthesized photocatalysts is illustrated in Fig. 6 . In addition, according to the MS curve and the bandgap of NH2-MIL-101(Fe), the calculated CB and VB of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) was -0.93 and 0.58 eV vs. NHE, respectively, and the CB (-0.49 eV) and VB (2.28 eV) of OH-GQDs was based on our previous literature [16].

Based on the band structures, the potential mechanisms for charges transfer were suggested and analyzed in Fig. 7 . Due to the OGNM-3 could convert CO2 into CO, and the CB of OH-GQDs was higher than the CO2/CO reduction potential, thus the Z-type heterojunction mechanism was proposed. The photogenerated electrons formed on the CB of OH-GQDs can rapidly migrate to the VB of NH2-MIL-101(Fe) after photoexcitation. Finally, there were photogenerated electrons remaining from NH2-MIL-101(Fe) that efficiently convert CO2 to CO, and the oxidation reaction is facilitated by the holes present in OH-GQDs. This unique Z-scheme catalytic nanostructure not only ensures effective spatial separation of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs but also carries out the redox capacity of the composite components. According to the above analysis, the possible CO2 photocatalytic reduction pathway by OGNM composites is analyzed as follows (refer to equations 1–4), the “CO2 ab” represents the adsorption state on the catalyst surface.
(1) CO2 + OH-GQDs/NH2-MIL-101(Fe) → CO2 ab
(2) OH-GQDs/NH2-MIL-101(Fe) + hv → h+ + e-
(3) H2O → 2e- + 1/2O2 + 2H+
(4) CO2 ab + 2e- + 2 h+ → CO

First, CO2 is massively adsorbed on the surface of the composite catalyst OGNM-3 (Eq. 1). Subsequently, due to the enhanced visible light absorption performance due to the introduction of OH-GQDs, when the binary catalyst is excited by visible light, photogenerated electrons and holes are excited from CB and VB, respectively (Eq. 2). Simultaneously, upon irradiation with visible light, both the amine-functionalized organic linker and Fe3-μ3-oxo clusters in the NH2-MIL-101 (Fe) structure undergo excitation, allows promote the mutual transformation of Fe3+ and Fe2+ and capture photogenerated electrons, which provides more electrons for transfer to the reduction of CO2 [18]. Finally, H2O adsorbed on the catalyst surface is oxidized (Eq. 3) and CO2 reacts with photogenerated electrons to generate CO (Eq. 4). Therefore, the mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide molecules by OGNM catalyst is shown in Fig. 8 .

In this study, a novel binary photocatalyst OGNM was synthesized by a simple solvothermal method, where OH-GQDs was loaded onto NH2-MIL-101(Fe). The SEM and TEM images clearly revealed the OGNM-3 maintains the hexagonal micropillar crystal structure of NH2-MIL-101, and the loaded OH-GQDs were uniformly dispersed on the catalyst surface. The composite material fully bonded both the hydroxyl functional groups of OH-GQDs and the amino functional groups and metal sites of NH2-MIL-101 as confirmed by FT-IR and XPS analyses. BET measurements indicated that the composite catalyst exhibited a favorable surface area and pore structure, the CO2 absorption–desorption isotherms revealed effective adsorption potential at lower pressures. The photocatalyst/water/acetonitrile system operated under visible light, without the assistance of sacrificial agents such as triethanolamine. The OGNM-3 catalyst efficiently photocatalytically reduced CO2 to CO, which was 5.3 times that of pure NH2-MIL-101(Fe), while MIL-101(Fe) without the amino functional groups did not exhibit any reactivity in this system. UV–vis-DRS and electrochemical characterizations indicated that the introduction of OH-GQDs enhanced the visible light responsiveness, promoting excitation of the amine-functionalized moieties and metal clusters within the NH2-MIL-101(Fe) structure. The obtained OGNM-3 nanocomposite formed a Z-type heterojunction to boost the photocatalytic efficiency according to their analysis of band structures. After the photocatalytic reaction, the characterizations of the catalyst demonstrated its stability. Overall, OGNM-3, as a binary photocatalyst, demonstrates the promising potential for visible light applications, offering an environmentally conscious strategy for tackling the pressing global agenda of sustainable carbon dioxide reduction solutions.

Haiyang Shao: Data Analysis. Fei Li: Data curation. Xiaodong Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Jianqiu Lei: Writing – review & editing. Ning Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
